feedback.text: I respectfully request consideration for the inclusion of video games particularly online and live-service titles within the scope of the Act. As digital products, games are subject to the same vulnerabilities of sudden deactivation, server shutdowns, and artificial obsolescence as other software. The growing frustration among consumers is best exemplified by the Stop Killing Games initiative, a petition supported by over a million gamers demanding legislative protections against the premature shutdown of video games that they legally own. This movement highlights a critical issue: many modern games, even those with single-player components, are being rendered unplayable when publishers choose to shut down servers or remove access. Inclusion of video games under the Digital Fairness Act would help ensure: That single-player content remains accessible even after server shutdowns; That publishers provide transparency and reasonable notice before delisting or disabling games; And that consumers have recourse when digital products are effectively killed. As video games become increasingly digital and interconnected, they must be afforded the same consumer protections expected from other forms of software and digital media. The inclusion of video games in the European Digital Fairness Act would not only support the principles of ownership and access, but also send a strong message against the growing trend of planned digital obsolescence in entertainment media. Sincerely, A concerned European gamer and consumer rights advocate
